content.text: Conflict between stakeholders in a GenAI programme is not an exception — it is the default condition. Conflicts arise from genuine differences in priorities (accuracy vs cost), information asymmetry (legal does not know what the AI actually sends to the model provider), organisational boundaries (the AI team does not understand the operational SLA; the operations lead does not understand confidence thresholds), and change anxiety (existing system owners perceive the AI as a threat). The question is not how to prevent all conflict but how to detect it early, classify it correctly, and resolve it through the right mechanism before it blocks a gate or a deployment. Unresolved conflict that reaches a funding gate kills programmes that would otherwise deliver value.

attrs.alt: Four GenAI conflict types: Technical vs Business, Legal vs Product, Operations vs AI Team, Sponsor vs Finance

content.text: Conflict Typology

GenAI programmes generate a predictable set of conflict types. Classifying the conflict correctly determines the resolution mechanism — applying a budget resolution process to an accountability conflict will not work, and vice versa.
Type 1: Accuracy Expectation Conflict

The most common technical conflict. The AI team defines accuracy using model evaluation metrics (precision, recall, F1 on a held-out test set). Operational stakeholders define accuracy using operational reliability standards ('the system must be correct at least 97% of the time or we cannot rely on it'). These are not the same measurement, and neither party is wrong — they are measuring different things for different purposes.
Root cause

— Accuracy thresholds were not agreed before build. The AI team built to maximise evaluated accuracy; the operations team assumed the AI would match the reliability standard of a human adjuster with 10 years of experience.
Resolution mechanism

— Agree accuracy thresholds and their measurement methodology before build begins — at the feasibility gate, not at the pilot review. If the conflict arises post-build, confidence routing is usually the resolution: the AI auto-classifies on high-confidence predictions (where accuracy is 94%+) and routes low-confidence predictions to human review. The operational SLA is met on auto-classified volume; the overall volume of AI-assisted decisions increases.
Preventive measure

— Include the operational definition of 'accuracy' in the use case requirements document. 'The AI must achieve 97% accuracy on standard motor claims, with complex liability claims routed to human review' is an unambiguous requirement. 'The AI must be accurate' is a conflict waiting to happen.
Type 2: Data Governance Conflict

Legal or the DPO objects to the data processing approach: the type of data being sent to the model, the consent basis, the model provider's data handling terms, or the geographic routing of data. This conflict is almost always preventable if legal is engaged at feasibility. When it arises during build or pilot, it is a symptom of late engagement.
Resolution mechanism

— Never ask legal to approve a conceptual 'AI system'. Give them an architecture diagram that shows exactly what data flows from which system to which endpoint, what transformation happens at the gateway (PII redaction, anonymisation), and what the model provider's data processing terms are. Most legal objections dissolve when the actual data flow is visible — the objection was to the imagined worst-case scenario, not the actual architecture.
Common specific conflicts

— 'We cannot send claimant PII to a US-based model API' (resolution: EU endpoint + DPA); 'We do not have consent to use this data for AI' (resolution: review actual consent terms with legal; restructure as processing under legitimate interests if applicable); 'The model provider might train on our data' (resolution: enterprise agreement with training opt-out).
Unresolvable cases

— If the use case fundamentally requires processing data that cannot be legally processed by any available model (e.g. certain categories of health data under strict sectoral regulation), the conflict is a legitimate kill signal, not a conflict to resolve. Document it and stop.
Type 3: Process Exclusion Conflict

An operational lead or department discovers they were not involved in the requirements process and are now being asked to accept a system designed without their input. This is a process failure, not a substantive conflict about the AI's design — but it generates substantive conflict because the excluded party redirects their frustration into objections about the AI's design.
How to recognise it

— The conflict is disproportionate to the specific objection: a technically minor concern ('the classification categories don't match our internal taxonomy') generates escalating opposition that the AI team cannot resolve through technical discussion. The underlying issue is not the taxonomy — it is the exclusion.
Resolution mechanism

— Acknowledge the process failure explicitly and without deflection. 'You should have been involved in the requirements phase and you weren't — that was a mistake we need to correct.' Hold a dedicated session to review decisions made and identify what can be revisited. Revise the governance structure to include the stakeholder in the ongoing programme. Symbolic inclusion (adding them to a distribution list) will not resolve the underlying trust deficit.
Prevention

— The stakeholder map produced during discovery is the prevention. Every stakeholder in the 'Manage Closely' quadrant must be involved in requirements design, not just informed of the outcomes. Inform-only engagement for high-power, high-interest stakeholders is the single most common cause of process exclusion conflicts.
Type 4: Budget and ROI Assumption Conflict

Finance or the CFO objects to the assumptions underlying the business case. Adoption rates are 'too optimistic'; FTE redeployment assumptions are 'not how this company works'; the 3-year horizon is 'too long for a technology that will be obsolete in 18 months'. The conflict is a legitimate challenge to the financial model's credibility.
Resolution mechanism

— Run the sensitivity analysis jointly. Invite Finance to challenge each assumption and produce their own bear-case estimate. The goal is a bear-case NPV that both parties agree is credible, not an NPV that optimises for approval. A business case that survives Finance's challenge has more internal credibility than one that was never tested.
Common sticking points

— Adoption rate: Finance often applies a 40–50% rate where the programme assumes 80%. Resolution: present comparable AI deployment case studies from similar organisations; agree a phased adoption curve (Year 1: 40%, Year 2: 65%, Year 3: 80%) rather than a flat assumption. FTE redeployment: Finance often refuses to count headcount avoidance as a benefit unless there is an explicit HR plan. Resolution: add a redeployment plan to the business case with sign-off from HR.
Conflict Register and Resolution Model

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

ConflictType = Literal[
    "accuracy_expectation",     # technical vs business expectation mismatch
    "data_governance",          # legal/privacy vs product team
    "process_exclusion",        # operational team not consulted
    "budget_assumption",        # ROI/finance disagreement
    "accountability_gap",       # who is responsible when AI is wrong
    "scope_creep",              # stakeholder adding requirements post-gate
    "turf_protection",          # existing system owner resists AI replacement
]

EscalationLevel = Literal["bilateral", "facilitated", "sponsor", "executive"]

@dataclass
class StakeholderConflict:
    id: str
    conflict_type: ConflictType
    parties: list[str]              # stakeholders in conflict
    description: str
    is_blocking: bool               # does this block a gate or deployment?
    days_unresolved: int = 0
    resolution_owner: str = ""      # who is responsible for driving resolution
    proposed_resolution: str = ""
    status: Literal["open", "in_progress", "resolved", "escalated"] = "open"

    @property
    def escalation_level(self) -> EscalationLevel:
        if self.days_unresolved <= 5 and not self.is_blocking:
            return "bilateral"      # parties can resolve directly
        if self.days_unresolved <= 10 or not self.is_blocking:
            return "facilitated"    # Change Lead or AI Lead facilitates
        if self.days_unresolved <= 20:
            return "sponsor"        # Executive sponsor involvement required
        return "executive"          # Board/ExCo level; programme may be at risk

    def resolution_playbook(self) -> str:
        playbooks = {
            "accuracy_expectation": (
                "Pre-align on accuracy metrics and thresholds BEFORE build. "
                "If conflict arises post-build: agree on a confidence routing strategy "
                "(high-confidence AI auto-classifies; low-confidence routes to human) "
                "that meets the operational SLA without requiring 97%+ accuracy on all inputs."
            ),
            "data_governance": (
                "Map the specific data elements causing concern. Propose PII redaction "
                "at the gateway, synthetic data for development, and a structured DPA review "
                "session with legal and the model provider's compliance team. "
                "Never ask legal to approve a vague 'AI solution' — give them an architecture diagram."
            ),
            "process_exclusion": (
                "Acknowledge the process failure without deflecting. "
                "Hold a dedicated session with the excluded stakeholder to review decisions made, "
                "identify which (if any) can be revisited, and revise the governance structure "
                "to include them going forward. Do not offer symbolic inclusion "
                "(distribution list additions) — offer real decision input."
            ),
            "budget_assumption": (
                "Run the sensitivity analysis jointly with the Finance stakeholder, not independently. "
                "Give them full visibility of the assumption log and invite them to challenge "
                "each input. The goal is a bear-case NPV that both parties agree is credible — "
                "not an NPV that optimises for approval."
            ),
            "accountability_gap": (
                "Establish the accountability model in writing before go-live: "
                "AI recommends; named role reviews; named role is accountable for the decision. "
                "Embed this in the product UX (confirmation step, audit log entry) "
                "and in the operational process documentation."
            ),
            "scope_creep": (
                "Return to the approved scope document and change control process. "
                "New requirements after Gate 3 require a scope change request, "
                "impact assessment on timeline and cost, and re-approval from the sponsor. "
                "Do not accommodate scope additions informally — "
                "they destroy project economics and timeline predictability."
            ),
            "turf_protection": (
                "Distinguish between legitimate concerns (the AI genuinely cannot do what the "
                "existing system does; transition risk is real) and political resistance "
                "(power or budget threat). Address legitimate concerns with specific evidence. "
                "Political resistance requires sponsor intervention: "
                "the existing system owner's concerns need to be addressed by "
                "the executive who sponsors both systems."
            ),
        }
        return playbooks.get(self.conflict_type, "No standard playbook — escalate to Change Lead.")

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"[{self.id}] {self.conflict_type} | Parties: {self.parties} | "
            f"Blocking: {self.is_blocking} | Days open: {self.days_unresolved} | "
            f"Escalation: {self.escalation_level} | Status: {self.status}"
        )


# Example conflict register for FNOL pilot
conflicts = [
    StakeholderConflict(
        id="C-01",
        conflict_type="accuracy_expectation",
        parties=["Head of Claims Operations", "AI Lead"],
        description="Ops requires 97% classification accuracy on all claim types. AI team's evaluation shows 89% overall, 94% on standard claims, 76% on complex liability claims.",
        is_blocking=True,
        days_unresolved=8,
        resolution_owner="AI Lead",
        proposed_resolution="Agree confidence routing: >90% confidence AI classifies automatically; <90% routes to adjuster. Effective accuracy on auto-classified volume = 94%.",
        status="in_progress",
    ),
    StakeholderConflict(
        id="C-02",
        conflict_type="data_governance",
        parties=["Legal Counsel", "Product Team"],
        description="Legal objects to sending claimant free-text (which may include personal details) to the model provider's API without an enterprise DPA.",
        is_blocking=True,
        days_unresolved=12,
        resolution_owner="AI Lead",
        proposed_resolution="PII redaction at gateway layer before API call; DPA in negotiation with model provider; legal review of architecture diagram.",
        status="in_progress",
    ),
    StakeholderConflict(
        id="C-03",
        conflict_type="turf_protection",
        parties=["CRM System Owner", "AI Programme"],
        description="CRM owner claims FNOL classification is already handled by CRM workflow rules and the AI project duplicates this.",
        is_blocking=False,
        days_unresolved=3,
        resolution_owner="Change Lead",
        proposed_resolution="Demo showing CRM rule accuracy vs AI accuracy on 200 historical cases. CRM rules achieve 72% vs AI 89%.",
        status="open",
    ),
]

for c in conflicts:
    print(c)
    print(f"  Playbook: {c.resolution_playbook()[:100]}...")
    print()
Escalation Protocol

Conflicts that are not resolved at the bilateral level within 5 working days need a defined escalation path. The protocol below applies to all conflict types; the specific resolution mechanism is conflict-type dependent:
Stage
Mechanism
Who Facilitates
Timeline
Trigger to Escalate
Stage 1: Bilateral
The two parties (or the two teams) attempt direct resolution using the resolution playbook for the conflict type
Parties self-manage
Days 1–5
No agreement after 5 days, or either party is a blocker who will not engage
Stage 2: Facilitated
Change Lead or AI Lead facilitates a structured session: each party states their position, then their interest (underlying need), then proposes options
Change Lead or AI Lead
Days 6–10
No agreement after facilitated session, or conflict is blocking a programme gate
Stage 3: Sponsor
Executive sponsor hears each party's position and interest, makes a binding decision, or refers to a named decision authority
Executive Sponsor
Days 11–20
Sponsor cannot resolve, or conflict involves parties at sponsor's peer level
Stage 4: Executive
ExCo or Board risk committee involvement; programme may be paused pending resolution
Programme Director + ExCo sponsor
Days 21+
This stage indicates a systemic governance failure in the programme — investigate root cause, not just the specific conflict

The escalation protocol is not a threat mechanism — it is a schedule that gives each party visibility of what happens if bilateral resolution fails. Parties who know that unresolved conflict escalates to their executive sponsor tend to engage more seriously at Stage 1 than they would in an open-ended process.
Governance Structures That Prevent Conflict Accumulation
Unresolved conflicts accumulate when there is no regular forum to surface and address them. Three governance mechanisms reduce conflict accumulation in GenAI programmes:
Steering Committee with Conflict Agenda Item

The programme's steering committee (typically monthly) should have a standing agenda item: 'Open conflicts and blockers'. Each open conflict is reviewed by status (open, in progress, escalated), the current escalation level is confirmed, and the resolution owner reports progress. If a conflict has been open for more than 10 working days without progress, the steering committee chair decides whether to escalate.
Technical Design Authority

Technical conflicts (accuracy expectations, integration architecture, data governance architecture) are often unresolvable in general stakeholder meetings because neither party has the relevant technical expertise. A Technical Design Authority (TDA) — typically the AI Lead, Platform Lead, and a nominated subject matter expert from the affected business area — can resolve technical conflicts through evidence: the evaluation results, the architecture diagram, the latency measurements. Decisions from the TDA are binding unless overturned by the executive sponsor.
Legal/Ethics Pre-Clearance Process

Rather than bringing legal and DPO concerns to the steering committee as conflicts, structure their involvement as a pre-clearance process: every use case that involves personal data or automated decisions goes through a documented legal/ethics review before Gate 4. The output is a clearance document (approved with conditions, or not approved with reasons). This converts reactive conflict into a proactive review that both the product team and legal can plan for.
Scope Creep and Turf Protection Conflicts
Scope Creep Conflict

Scope creep in GenAI programmes typically originates from stakeholders who were satisfied with the approved scope at Gate 3 but, once the programme is underway, identify additional requirements they 'forgot' to raise or that emerged from seeing early demo versions of the product. Each individual addition seems small; cumulatively they destroy the programme economics and timeline.
Pattern

— A stakeholder requests that the FNOL classification tool also generate a first-draft claim summary (a different AI capability requiring different evaluation and build effort); another requests integration with a legacy system that was out of scope; a third requests a mobile interface for field adjusters. Each request, if accommodated without a change control process, consumes capacity without corresponding budget.
Resolution

— The scope change request (SCR) process is the resolution. Every requirement added after Gate 3 approval must go through an SCR that documents: what is being added, the impact on timeline and cost, which approved requirement (if any) is being deprioritised to accommodate it, and sign-off from the sponsor. An SCR that requires sponsor sign-off dramatically reduces casual scope additions while preserving a legitimate path for genuine new requirements.
Governance prevention
— The requirements baseline signed at Gate 3 is the reference document. Every design review should open by confirming that the deliverable aligns with the baseline. Any deviation triggers an SCR.
Turf Protection Conflict

Turf protection conflicts arise when the owner or team responsible for an existing system perceives the AI programme as a threat to their system's relevance, budget, or their team's headcount. The conflict typically manifests as technical objections ('the AI can't handle our edge cases') that are disproportionate to the actual technical limitations.
How to distinguish from legitimate technical concern

— A legitimate technical concern is specific ('the AI misclassifies claims with multiple loss types at a 40% error rate — here are 15 examples') and has not previously been raised by the same person in non-technical forums. A turf protection objection is general ('this AI will never be as good as our system'), is raised primarily in management forums rather than technical reviews, and is not accompanied by specific reproducible examples.
Resolution for legitimate concerns

— Run a structured comparison on the existing system's specific edge case examples. If the AI performs worse on those cases, acknowledge it, scope them out, and document the limitation. A use case with a narrower but well-defined scope that is delivered is more valuable than a use case with an unlimited scope that is never deployed.
Resolution for political resistance

— Sponsor involvement is required. The existing system owner's concerns need to be addressed by the executive who sponsors both systems — whether that is a shared budget discussion, a formal systems rationalisation programme, or an explicit commitment that the AI is complementary rather than replacement. The AI programme team cannot resolve this conflict because they are perceived as a party to it.
Accountability Conflict: Who Is Responsible When the AI Is Wrong?

The accountability conflict is the most legally and operationally significant conflict in GenAI programmes and is the one most commonly deferred rather than resolved. When the AI-assisted FNOL tool misclassifies a claim, causing the wrong policy section to be applied and the claim to be underpaid, who is accountable? The answer must be established before deployment, not after the first operational failure:
Define the accountability model in writing

— AI recommends; [named role] reviews and confirms or overrides; [named role] is accountable for the decision outcome. This model must appear in the product design (the confirmation step), the operational procedure, and the audit log.
Ensure the review step is genuine, not performative

— If the 'human review' step is designed to be completed in 2 seconds with no ability to see the underlying reasoning, it is a compliance theatre checkbox, not a genuine review. The human reviewer must have access to the AI's confidence score, the evidence it used to classify the claim, and a straightforward mechanism to override. Regulators who investigate AI-related customer harm will examine whether the human review step was genuine.
Document the error handling procedure

— What happens when a misclassification is identified after the claim has been processed? Who is notified, what is the remediation procedure, and how is the affected customer compensated? These questions must be answered by Operations, Legal, and Compliance before go-live — not improvised at the point of the first error.
Agree the model failure threshold

— At what error rate does the AI stop being deployed and the process revert to fully manual? This threshold should be agreed at the steering committee before deployment and monitored via production metrics from day one. A programme without a pre-agreed failure threshold will struggle to make the kill decision when accuracy degrades, because the decision will always feel premature until a customer-facing failure makes it unavoidable.
Summary

GenAI programmes generate predictable conflict types: accuracy expectation mismatches, data governance objections, process exclusion reactions, budget assumption challenges, and accountability gaps. Each type has a resolution mechanism; applying the wrong mechanism (or no mechanism) produces stalemate.

The escalation protocol provides a time-bounded path from bilateral resolution (days 1–5) through facilitated sessions (days 6–10), sponsor involvement (days 11–20), and executive escalation (day 21+). Naming the path in advance improves bilateral resolution rates.

Three governance structures reduce conflict accumulation: a steering committee with a standing conflicts agenda item, a Technical Design Authority for technical conflicts, and a pre-clearance process for legal/ethics concerns.

The accountability conflict — who is accountable when the AI produces a wrong output affecting a customer — must be resolved before deployment, not deferred. The accountability model (AI recommends; named role reviews; named role is accountable) must be embedded in the product UX, the operational procedure, and the audit log.

Process exclusion conflicts are not about the AI's design — they are about a stakeholder who was not consulted feeling that a decision was made without them. The resolution is acknowledgement and genuine inclusion going forward, not technical discussion about the objection being raised.
